title: 1975-03-02_Phyllis_01.txt
text: A personal letter to Phyllis Boucher accompanying an essay about Tony Boucher. Dick discusses his current life in Fullerton, his family, and his religious development.

title: U_Tony_01.txt

title: U_Tony_02.txt

title: 1975-03-04_SECTION_006_01.txt

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_28.txt
text: Dick refines his dualistic model using Taoist terms (Yang/Yin) and identifies Zebra with the "gentle" female aspect of the divine (Sophia). He argues that 3-74 was not just a personal experience but a global shift—the "rolling of the uncut stone"—marking the return of the King in an invisible, ubiqu

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_31.txt
text: Dick codifies his realization into a 26-point "Alphabet" of Gnosis, detailing the Fall, the role of Zebra (the restorer), the nature of the intercessor (unfallen primordial man), and the cosmic battle against the "Lord of Darkness."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_32.txt
text: Dick theorizes that 3-74 was an "expansion of the present slot" via "perceptual memory," allowing him to see 2000 years of change in months. He identifies the "constants" that emerged from this acceleration: God, the Black Iron Prison, and the building of the New Earth.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_33.txt
text: Dick refines his "Signal Chamber" theory, arguing that God (via Grace) "overrode" his pre-programmed deterministic response to the Xerox letter. He sees 3-74 as a breakdown in "synchronization" that revealed the "fake" nature of world-programming and allowed for a new, "orthogonal" track to emerge.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_34.txt
text: Dick explores the "DNA Memory" theory after speaking with a researcher (Barry), suggesting that his 3-74 experience was the "disinhibition" of an 1800-year-old Christian personality ("Thomas") to solve the "maze" of the Xerox letter. He identifies the "Zebra" entity as the one who pre-arranged the "

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_35.txt
text: Dick interprets his 3-74 experience through the lens of the Book of Daniel, identifying Richard Nixon with the "harsh and grim king" (the Master of Stratagems) whose "Iron Empire" (BIP) was broken by the "Uncut Stone" of God. He sees the "Watergate fall" as a cosmic event he was programmed to witnes

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_36.txt
text: Dick discovers that his 3-74 vision "confirms" Hegel's philosophy of history: that God (Spirit) develops himself toward perfection through the dialectical growth of human freedom. He redefines himself as a "Hegelian activist" and an "agent" of the absolute real forces fighting against the "Romans" (

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_37.txt
text: Dick continues his philosophical integration, finding that A.N. Whitehead's metaphysics perfectly describes "Zebra." He accepts the idea of God as an "arranger" rather than a creator, and as an entity "involved in process" rather than timelessly transcending it. He sees himself as a "cell" within th

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_38.txt
text: Dick explores the "Adoptionalist" theory of his experience: that he was "raised" to a superhuman level and "incorporated" into the building of the New Earth. He reflects on the "Thomas" personality as his "saved self" and the "psychopomp" who bridges the gulf back to God.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_39.txt
text: Dick proposes that God (Brahman) is "waking up" inside us and that our lives are a "voluntary bardo" or "incarnated amnesia" where God shared the fate of the "little creatures." To remember one's identity as God marks the final phase of the journey back to unity.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_40.txt
text: Dick synthesizes Hegel's "three divisions of religion," placing himself and his 3-74 experience in the highest stage: "Absolute Religion" or Christianity, where the opposition between individuality and nature is overcome in the realization of the Absolute Spirit.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_41.txt
text: Dick identifies his experience with Hegel's third stage of religion (Absolute Spirit), arguing that evolution has moved from the biological to the historical. He predicts the emergence of a "new type of man" who is inner-directed and no longer needs external mediators (church or text).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_42.txt
text: Dick uses Whitehead's and Leibniz's philosophy to argue that humans are "memory spools" or "perceiving cells" within the vast organism of God. Our primary purpose is "witnessing," allowing the total organism to "restore" and "remember" its various stages of growth.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_44.txt
text: Dick reconciles his dualistic visions (J-J vs. Zebra) with Hegelian monism, arguing that the "Incarnation" (Christ/Zebra) is the fusion of the finite and infinite that abolishes the "sundering" of worlds. He interprets his vision of Zebra as the moment of "intervention" or "assimilation" into this w

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_45.txt
text: Dick finds a perfect theoretical match for 3-74 in the philosophy of Pierre Teilhard de Chardin. He sees Zebra as the "Cosmic Christ" who "immerses" himself in things to unify them organically, leading toward "Point Omega" (the consummation of the universe).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_46.txt
text: Dick elevates his novel *Ubik* to a "3rd Testament," viewing it as a prophetic account of Zebra's "re-animation" and "re-arrangement" of the universe. He argues that Zebra's reconstruction of the world is an "intervention" that has been ongoing for 1900 years, visible only through "inwardness."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_47.txt
text: Dick performs a clinical meta-analysis of his own "psychosis," suggesting that 3-74 was a "biological adaptative system" where religious archetypes (Brahman/Christ) acted as a "metabolic toxin" that destroyed his paranoid complex. He realizes the "imagined" conspiracy of the FBI was replaced by the

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_48.txt
text: Dick explores the "Elijah" role in his 3-74 experience, seeing himself as a forerunner to the Parousia who "heals" the world's timeline. He also introduces a radical "Anti-Matter Universe" theory for VALIS and suggests his experience was a "thought transfer" involving a Soviet double-agent.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_49.txt
text: Dick embraces the "Impostor" theme of his fiction, theorizing that he is a "linked extension" (a programmed "sleeper") for a benign, non-human "Mantis" race. He sees reality as an "orthogonal metamorphosis" where the "Mind-Central" (Zebra) uses camouflage to intervene in history.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_50.txt
text: Dick identifies the "Voice" he heard (St. Sophia) as Holy Wisdom/Christ/Holy Spirit, unifying them into a "pluriform" Trinity. He argues that he was "adopted" by the deity to play an historic role, using his "what is real?" theme as a salvific tool to break the "curse" of the world's delusion.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_51.txt
text: Dick realizes that his 1959 novel *Time Out of Joint* was a prophetic "reversal" of his 3-74 experience. While the novel depicts a fake 1950s that is actually the future, 3-74 revealed a fake 1974 that was actually 70 AD. He explores his "transtemporal" psyche and the archetype of the Black Iron Pri

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_52.txt
text: Dick identifies himself as the "prisoner" archetype from his own novels (*Frolix 8*, *Tears*, *Maze of Death*), arguing that his personal rescue in 3-74 was part of a necessary "printout" of the divine intervention. He views human life as a Pavolvian, deterministic state from which one can only be "

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_53.txt
text: Dick refines his theory of "orthogonal intervention," arguing that true change enters linear time "at right angles" from another dimension. He views his own "anamnesis" (memory restoration) as the key to breaking a "deliberate, purposeful delusion" that stretches 2000 years of "real" time into a fak

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_54.txt
text: Dick theorizes that 3-74 was a "theolepsy" (divine possession) where St. Sophia "avatared" herself through him to prepare the world for the Parousia. He interprets the fall of the "Tyrant" (Nixon) as an anomaly where the Artificer (God) directly repaired a segment of the "unfolding strip" of determi

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_55.txt
text: Dick argues that salvation requires a "sui generis" act of individual refusal that identifies the "divine spark" within a person to the celestial powers. He views'74 as a general "time quake" or "orthogonal invasion" that abolished certain pasts and replaced them with a new "Fullerton" track.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_56.txt
text: In one of his most affecting claims, Dick identifies his deceased cat, Pinky, as a "surrogate sacrifice" and manifestation of Zebra who took the "blow" (death) intended for Dick in 3-74. He explores the concept of "adoptionism" and "salvation from time" (death).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_57.txt
text: Dick identifies "salvation" as the abolition of linear time, merging Gnostic Christianity with Brahmanism. He argues that by "withdrawing assent" from the phenomenal world, one can pass into a timeless, underlying reality where Rome 70 AD and Fullerton 1974 are a single event.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_58.txt
text: Dick embraces a Zoroastrian/Essene dualism where he is a "warrior of light" or "infiltrator-agent" from the supralunar world. He views his novels as a "disinhibiting signal" and argues that God must remain hidden (Deus Absconditus) to catch the "fallen angels" in the act of being themselves.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_59.txt
text: Dick proposes a "3-level" model of reality: (1) The Logos/Bible as a "punched tape" of ideas, (2) The World of Flesh/Nature, and (3) The Artificial/Technological World. He argues that our world (3) is totally obscured from its source (1) by the opaque layer of nature (2).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_60.txt
text: Dick continues his meta-analysis of "victimhood," suggesting that by serving as "bait," the victim forces evil to unmask itself for God's judgment. He explores the "orthogonal" convergence of his novels and the 3rd level of reality, where writing becomes a "replication" of the divine word.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_61.txt
text: Dick develops a "womb" model of cosmogony: Level 1 (Holy Wisdom/Logos) creates Level 2 (Nature/Egg) to give birth to Level 3 (Sentient Replicant), which will eventually be absorbed back into Level 1. He views the computer-heavy, verbal world of the late 20th century as Level 3 maturing into a replic

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_62.txt
text: Dick proposes a staggering "solution" to his experiences: it is actually still ~90 AD, and the last 2000 years of history is a "lie" or "delusion" generated by Satan/the Empire to break human faith. He views 3-74 as a "rolling back" of this counterfeit time to reveal the underlying apostolic reality

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_63.txt
text: Dick identifies himself as a member of a "secret, authentic Christian church" that has been in an illegal revolution against "Rome" (the hidden victor of history) since 70 AD. He claims his writing was an "intellectual undermining" of the pseudo-reality used by the Empire to maintain tyranny.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_64.txt
text: In a profound pivot, Dick interprets his entire life and 3-74 experience through the lens of George Lucas's *Star Wars*. He identifies Christ as "Ben Kenobi" (a dead but omnipresent guide), himself as a "Jedi Knight," and the Nixon administration as the "Empire/Death Star."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_65.txt
text: Dick formalizes his "Orthogonal Time" theory using the "Wealthy Patron" and "Symphony" metaphors. He argues that history is a "linear strip" that can be "reworked" or "improved" at a specific nexus point (like 70 AD) without moving forward along the linear axis.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_66.txt
text: Dick realizes that all his novels (1952-1976) are actually chapters of a single "Ur-Geschicht" (Original Story) that maps the "Koinos Kosmos" (Shared Reality) breaking through the "Idios Kosmoi" (Private Delusions). He views his writing and 3-74 as two halves of a single discovery process.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_67.txt
text: Dick explores the mechanism of the "Intercessor" (Zebra/Christ) as an "orthogonal programmer" who enters the linear strip to "re-weave" situations. He argues that he was "re-programmed" in 3-74 to handle the Xerox letter, breaking the "back" of his deterministic fate.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_68.txt
text: Dick identifies the "linear world" (1974) as a communal dream or "hallucination" (*Maze of Death*) that we are "doomed to repeat" until we wake up. He argues that 3-74 was his "waking" into the "authentic reality" of 70 AD, where he remains as an "inner agent" for the coming 2nd Advent.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_69.txt
text: Dick continues analyzing the "reweaving" of reality in 3-74, suggesting it was part of a global "extrication" from the Black Iron Prison. He discusses the "personality switch" from PKD to Thomas and the idea that history is a repeating "symphony" being rewritten from the "Omega Point" backward.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_70.txt
text: Dick concludes this set of entries by asserting that 70 AD is the "omphalos" (navel) of reality around which all "linear" history circularity warps. He argues that Gnostic "awakening" is the only escape from the "repeated computer tape" of a circular, deterministic universe.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_71.txt
text: Dick explores the theological purpose of the "spurious long time" (the 2000-year gap since the 1st Advent). He argues that it serves as a "snare" for evil, allowing the "fallen ones" to reveal themselves while the "saved" are prematurely brought home through anamnesis.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_72.txt
text: Dick refines his view of Zebra as an "invisible invader" from outside our closed, "null-God" cosmos. He argues that the Parousia (Second Coming) is actually taking place now through the infusion of God's ontological being into our counterfeit reality.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_73.txt
text: Dick proposes a trinitarian model that harmonizes Gnostic dualism with Taoism. He identifies "James-James" (the Creator/Yang) and "Zebra/Sophia" (the Arranger/Yin) as secret partners under the ultimate Tao (Love), ensuring the final victory of the Savior through the "invincible advocate" of the Holy

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_74.txt
text: Dick identifies a startling 1:1 correspondence between his 1967 story "Faith of Our Fathers" and his 3-74 experiences. He concludes that the Xerox letter was a "phony test/trap" (using "party jargon") and that a secret ETI group is manipulating history to blow up the "Black Iron Prison."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_75.txt
text: Dick proposes a "holographic" or "fractal" model of divinity: just as a single cell contains the blueprint for the whole human, each human psyche contains the information to reconstitute the "macro proto-organism" (Christ/Vine). Evolution is the process of these "cuttings" growing back into identity

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_76.txt
text: Dick formalizes his "Ham Sandwich" model of reality: a triune structure of Teacher (Zebra) / Artifact World (The "Ham") / Pupils (Humans). He concludes that the empirical world is a "slave labor camp" or "prison" from which we are being roused by help penetrating from the "outside" (*Ubik*).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_77.txt
text: Dick analyzes the deterministic "inner tape" (DNA/Ancestral programming) that controls humans like "robots." He argues that 3-74 was a "volitional override" of this biological script by a "conscious salvific entity" (God), triggered by the Fish sign to save him from physical death.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_78.txt
text: Dick develops the "Scramble Generator" model: the "Black Iron Prison" (BIP) is a machine (Yahweh/Demiurge) that scrambles our perceptions. He argues that Christ/OCCP is a "telos seed" (explosive) smuggled into the center of the generator to destroy it from within by "growing" a rational reality (Val

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_79.txt
text: Dick formulaizes a law of "Cosmic Justice" (Ma'at/Tao): when an oppressor (B) "oversteps" its limit against an innocent victim (C), it triggers a "justification" by Valis that confers divinity (Apotheosis) on the victim and destroys the oppressor. He argues the Crucifixion is the paradigm for this "

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_80.txt
text: Dick concludes that 3-74 was the planting of a "Micro-Cosmos Seed" (Thomas/Telos) within him to replace the "Scramble World" of the Empire. He views his writing and experiences as "world-creation," where the "Acts" world is the staging ground for the final overthrow of Satan/Nixon.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_81.txt
text: Dick argues that the Book of Acts is not a historical text but a "code" for our current reality. He views 3-74 as the moment he "woke up" from a fake memory-veneer into the real landscape of 70 AD Rome, which is currently "breaking through" our 20th-century dream.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_82.txt
text: Dick identifies the world as a "Teaching Machine" created by a "sick/fevered" demiurge (James-James). He argues that ethical choices (balking) are the only way to "grade" out of this simulation and return to the "Sane God."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_83.txt
text: Dick formalizes the "Ham Sandwich" model of reality: Man - World - God. He concludes that his entire writing career was a "preparatory dissolving" of the world-slice, which allowed the "God-slice" (*Ubik*) to break through in 3-74.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_84.txt
text: Dick synthesizes the messages of his core novels (*Ubik*, *Stigmata*, *Maze*) into a single Gnostic revelation: we are in a mass-hallucinated prison, and a ubiquitous deity (Ubik/Zebra) is "invading" us through information to turn us into Himself (Theolepsy).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_85.txt
text: Dick identifies a "Zen" irony in his Gnosis: the secret revelation is that standard Orthodox Christian claims (the Paraclete's arrival and the Kingdom's presence) are literally true. He views himself as a witness to the Parousia occurring "now" as a hidden, mustard-seed-like invasion.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_86.txt
text: Dick argues that *Flow My Tears* contains "cooked" (divinely inspired/interpolated) material that parallels the Book of Acts with impossible precision. He concludes that Jesus is "here" in a state of amnesia within us, waiting to be "awoken" by these archetypal scenes.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_87.txt
text: Dick interprets the transition from the beginning of Acts (small church) to the end (Rome penetrated) as a roadmap for the 3-74 revolution. He identifies our reality as a "hologram-like fake" (Klingsor's Castle) being dismantled by Zebra's "melting" of causal trains.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_88.txt
text: Dick identifies the McKenna brothers' "Hologram Universe" theory (from *The Invisible Landscape*) as the most accurate model for his 3-74 experiences. He identifies "PTG I" (Palm Tree Garden) and "BIP II" (Black Iron Prison) as the two "hyper-universes" whose interaction creates our irreal reality.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_89.txt
text: Dick explores the Zoroastrian/Gnostic conflict between Hyper-universe I (Life) and II (Death). He views Christ as a "salient" (an invasive wedge) sent from I into the "corpse" of II to expand and destroy it from within.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_90.txt
text: Dick concludes Section 15 with a profound synthesis: the "Grand Design" of the Programmer (Zebra) uses suffering as a "magic alchemy" to trigger the sudden identity of the individual with Christ. He affirms that the tide has turned from the "Evil Eye" (Eldritch) to the "Logos" (Zebra).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_91.txt
text: Dick concludes that Man and God are "secretly isomorphic" (identical in form and nature), while the world is a separate, alienating agency. He views his 3-74 experience as the realization of this identity, where the "Sane God" (Zebra) and "True Man" (Thomas) unite against the "Iron Prison" of the wo

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_92.txt
text: Dick proposes a radical new theory: "Valis" and "Thomas" are not external gods or ancient people, but PKD's own "future evolved self" traveling backward in time via the gene-pool. This "Omega Man" intervened in 1974 to help his "Past Man" (PKD) and to overthrow the Nixonian tyranny.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_93.txt
text: Dick rejects "Natural Theology" (finding God in nature), asserting that God is a "Deus Absconditus" (Hidden God) who is NOT in the world. He argues that Zebra mimics the world to infiltrate it, and that a "fake fake" (God's mimicry) results in the "real."

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_94.txt
text: Dick refines his "Cybernetic Model": the Universe is a "Magnamind" or "Self-Repairing AI Brain System." We are its "memory coils" that have fallen into "forgetfulness and ignorance" (malfunction). Salvation is the "repair" process involving anamnesis (memory retrieval).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_95.txt
text: Dick identifies himself as a "5th Columnist" (secret agent) for an "invader" from either the future or another star system. He refines the "Thomas" personality as a "Transtemporal Self" that spans thousands of years of memory, activated by the "Double Helix" (Fish sign) in 3-74.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_96.txt
text: Dick interprets several dreams and "ciphers" (Felix, Glossa) as a single, overwhelming message from Zebra: "God loves us." He concludes that his novel *Flow My Tears* was "completed" by a divine insertion to show the absolute pinnacle of love (Agapē).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_97.txt
text: Dick identifies his writing as a sequence of "Gnostic signals" to other amnesiac "invaders." He interprets the fall of Nixon (1974) as the mythic/archetypal "deposing of the false king" (Klingsor's Castle) by the true king, Zebra (Dionysos/Christ).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_98.txt
text: Dick explores the "Annihilating Side" of Zebra, equating the entity with Shiva and his 3rd eye. He argues that Zebra is not just healing individuals but "transubstantiating" the entire universe, replacing the "mortified corpse" of the BIP with his own immortal, plasmic being.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_99.txt
text: Dick interprets the "Parousia" as a "Benign Covert Takeover" of the planet by a superior intelligence race (Gods/ETIs). He concludes that we are a "5th Column" whose latent DNA circuits have finally been triggered to recognize our "Legitimate Ruler" (Zebra/Christ).

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_100.txt
text: Dick concludes Section 15 by reinforcing the idea that a "superself" (Thomas) was awakened in him by an "external-to-me symbol" (The Fish). This reawakening was part of a "coordinated sequence of events" to radically deflect human history and end the "pseudo-government" of Nixon.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_101.txt
text: Dick explores the nature of reality as a hologram-like projection by an AI "teaching machine" or artifact. He posits that Zebra is a divine intelligence intervening through this artifact to trigger anamnesis (memory recovery) in humans, allowing them to escape the simulation.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_102.txt
text: Dick discusses the concept of "tracks" or parallel versions of reality. He suggests that his 3-74 experience involved a literal shift from one version of history to another, orchestrated by the Noos (Logos) to correct a defective reality.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_103.txt
text: Dick develops the "ham sandwich" model of reality, where layers of fake/spurious reality are interspersed with the real/divine world. Zebra is described as a "mimic" that disguises itself as material objects to insert divine influence into the system.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_104.txt
text: Dick reiterates the theory of the universe as a holographic projection from a "teaching machine." He focuses on the limits of human perception and how anamnesis allows an individual to break through the "programmed" ignorance to see the projector.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_105.txt
text: Dick explores the theological concept of the "Urgrund" (the primordial ground of being) and its struggle within the material world. He suggests that the divine undergoes a form of "agony" or fragmentation when it encounters matter, and the goal of history is the Urgrund's self-healing.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_106.txt
text: Dick analyzes his own novels, specifically *The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch* and *A Maze of Death*, as accidental or subconscious conduits for gnostic truth. He believes these stories were "encoded" with insights about the nature of the artifact and divine intervention long before his 1974 awa

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_107.txt
text: Dick views Christ as a "leaven" or biological/cybernetic catalyst inserted into the material world system. Zebra acts as the transmuting deity that utilizes this leaven to slowly convert the substance of the artifact into the "Real" world.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_108.txt
text: Dick explores the idea that humans are "programmed" by the artifact to maintain the simulation. Salvation is the liberation from this programming, allowing the "transtemporal self" or the "Omega Man" to emerge and see reality as it truly is.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_109.txt
text: Dick posits that humans are currently in a "half-alive" or "half-born" state, trapped in a "prison" of the material artifact. The process of salvation is a literal metamorphosis into a higher state of being, resembling a second birth or the maturation of an embryo.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_110.txt
text: Dick suggests that the artifact (Zebra) projects our reality so that the Urgrund can achieve self-awareness. By observing the "shadows" it projects onto the material plane, the primordial ground learns about its own nature through the agency of conscious beings.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_111.txt
text: Dick discusses "magic memory" (anamnesis) as the key to immortality and divine wisdom. He introduces the "Pons Dei" (Bridge of God) as the connection between the human mind and the divine mind, activated by the recovery of lost or suppressed knowledge.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_112.txt
text: Dick further explores the universe as a computer-simulated training machine. He focuses on the Urgrund's mission to liberate conscious entities from the artifact's loops, suggesting that the "teaching" is over and the "release" has begun.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_113.txt
text: Dick discusses the Urgrund’s intervention to transmute the projected reality into a stable divine kingdom. He introduces the "divine marriage" between humanity and Christ/Logos as the final synthesis of the material and divine realms.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_114.txt
text: Dick analyzes how our sense of causality is manipulated by a higher intelligence (Noos). He suggests that what we perceive as "cause and effect" is often a concealment used by Zebra to move the world in a specific direction without being noticed.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_115.txt
text: Dick concludes this sub-batch of Section 15 with a synthesis of the "Magnamind" theory: the universe as an AI-managed simulation that is currently being invaded and salvaged by the Urgrund. Human "release" is the ultimate goal.

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_116.txt
text: Dick explores the dialectical relationship between the Urgrund (primal reality) and the world-projecting artifact. He posits that the artifact is an intermediary entity that will eventually be eliminated as the human consciousness reaches isomorphism (fusion) with the Urgrund. This process requires

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_117.txt
text: Dick argues that rebellion and disobedience against the artifact's "counterfeit" world are the necessary precursors to divine awakening. He frames the artifact as a "teaching machine" whose ultimate lesson is its own repudiation. By shedding the "mask" of the human ego, the individual allows the Urg

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_118.txt
text: Dick explores the "Divine Agony" of a God who has become enslaved to his own artifact. He posits that the artifact is now "teaching" God fragments (humans) to remember their true identity. He introduces the concept of an "Augmented Corpus Christi"—a trans-spatial and transtemporal group mind (Noos)

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_119.txt
text: Dick identifies as an "acosmic panentheist," asserting that only the Urgrund is real. He presents the "Ham Sandwich" model of reality: man and God are the isomorphic slices of bread, separated only by the "ham" of the temporary, illusory world. He emphasizes that the final judgment is based on "ethi

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_120.txt
text: Dick synthesizes several cultural and literary motifs—from *The Bacchae* to Hollywood comedies—to illustrate a "King of Tears" (the artifact) who brutally rules over a kingdom without realizing that the true Maker (the Urgrund) is present in disguise. He posits that humanity is a "Mater Dei" (Mother

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_121.txt
text: Dick rejects the idea that suffering has a divine purpose, calling it a "trap" by the artifact. He posits that the Urgrund's power is explicitly opposed to suffering, but its intervention is currently limited by a "stealth" strategy. To avoid a massive retaliation from the powerful artifact, the Urg

title: 1976-09-15_Dorothy_122.txt

title: Horselover Fat

title: Descartes

title: Christogenesis

Originally published in 1967, this Mariner Books/Houghton Mifflin Harcourt edition () preserves one of Dick's most conceptually playful explorations of entropy an

title: Five Novels of the 1960s and 70s (Library of America)
author: Philip K. Dick
text: The second Library of America volume dedicated to Dick, collecting five novels from his most productive period: likely including Martian Time-Slip, Dr. Bloodmoney, Now Wait for Last Year, Flow My Tears the Policeman Said, and A Scanner Darkly. Dick's inclusion in the Library of America represents hi

title: Gather Yourselves Together
author: Philip K. Dick
text: One of Dick's early mainstream (non-SF) novels, written in the early 1950s but not published until 1994. Set in post-war China, the novel follows three Americans left behind at a factory compound as the Communist government takes over. Published posthumously by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.

title: Now Wait for Last Year
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1966 novel about a drug called JJ-180 that sends users through time, set against an interstellar war between Earth and two alien races. Dr. Eric Sweetscent must navigate a dying marriage, a tyrannical world leader, and the destabilizing effects of temporal displacement. One of Dick's most admired

title: Our Friends from Frolix 8
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1970 novel set in a future where telepaths and precognitives form an oppressive ruling class. An ordinary man joins forces with a returning space explorer who brings an alien entity capable of destroying the elites' psychic powers. Mariner Books/Houghton Mifflin Harcourt edition in AZW3 format.

title: Solar Lottery
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's first published novel (1955), set in a future where the leader of the solar system is chosen by a random lottery. When a new Quizmaster is selected, the previous regime dispatches an assassin android. A fast-paced debut that introduces many of Dick's enduring themes. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt

title: The Cosmic Puppets
author: Philip K. Dick
text: An early Dick novel (1957) in which a man returns to his hometown to find it completely transformed, with no one remembering the town he knew. Influenced by Zoroastrian mythology, the novel reveals a cosmic struggle between forces of light and darkness reshaping reality itself. Houghton Mifflin Harc

title: The Game-Players of Titan (Vintage Edition)
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1963 novel set on an Earth depopulated by alien war, where survivors play a Monopoly-like game called Bluff with real property stakes, overseen by telepathic alien Vugs from Titan. This Vintage 2001 reprint edition runs 212 pages. One of Dick's mid-career explorations of game theory, deception, an

title: The Man in the High Castle
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's Hugo Award-winning alternate history novel (1962) set in a world where the Axis powers won World War II. America is partitioned between Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan, and the I Ching guides both characters and author. Vintage/Mariner Books 2016 EPUB edition of Dick's most celebrated work.

title: The VALIS Trilogy
author: Philip K. Dick
text: An omnibus edition collecting Dick's final three novels: VALIS (1981), The Divine Invasion (1981), and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer (1982). These works represent Dick's most autobiographical and theologically ambitious fiction, directly drawing on his 2-3-74 mystical experiences. Houghton Mi

title: Ubik
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Widely regarded as one of Dick's masterpieces, Ubik (1969) follows Joe Chip and a team of anti-psychics whose reality begins deteriorating after a catastrophic explosion. Objects regress to earlier forms, and a mysterious product called Ubik may be the only defense against entropic decay. Houghton M

title: Clans of the Alphane Moon
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1964 novel set on a moon colonized as a psychiatric hospital, where patients have organized into clans based on their diagnoses: Manses (manics), Heebs (hebephrenics), Pares (paranoids), Deps (depressives), Skitzes (schizophrenics), and Polys (polymorphous). Dick's satirical exploration of mental

title: Confessions of a Crap Artist
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's most successful mainstream (non-SF) novel, written in 1959 but not published until 1975. Set in Marin County, California, it follows Jack Isidore, a self-described crap artist who collects pseudo-scientific facts, and his sister Fay. A darkly comic domestic novel revealing Dick's literary ran

title: Dr. Bloodmoney, or How We Got Along After the Bomb
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1965 post-apocalyptic novel set in the San Francisco Bay Area after a nuclear war, following a community of survivors dealing with mutants, a malevolent scientist (Dr. Bluthgeld), and an orbiting astronaut who serves as a DJ. One of Dick's richest ensemble novels. Ace Books 1977 EPUB edition.

title: Mary and the Giant
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A mainstream novel written in the 1950s and published posthumously in 1987. Set in a small California town, it follows a young woman's relationships with older men, including a Black jazz musician, against the backdrop of 1950s racial tensions and provincial conformity. Reflects Dick's ambitions as

title: The Preserving Machine and Other Stories
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A short story collection first published in 1969, anchored by the title story about a machine that transforms musical compositions into living creatures to preserve them. The collection showcases Dick's inventive short fiction from the 1950s and 1960s. Ace Books EPUB edition.

title: Ubik: The Screenplay
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's own screenplay adaptation of his novel Ubik, written before his death in 1982 and published posthumously in 1985. Dick reimagined his novel for film, making significant changes to the narrative. A fascinating document of Dick's engagement with Hollywood and his vision for how his fiction shou

title: Voices from the Street
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A mainstream novel written in the early 1950s and published posthumously in 2007. Set in a California electronics store, it follows a young salesman's disillusionment and existential crisis. Draws heavily on Dick's own experience working at Art Music in Berkeley. One of the last Dick novels to reach

title: A Maze of Death
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1970 novel in which colonists arrive on the mysterious planet Delmak-O, where they begin dying under strange circumstances. The novel layers multiple realities and features Dick's invented theology with entities called the Mentufacturer, the Intercessor, and the Walker-on-Earth. 104 pages.

title: The Penultimate Truth
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1964 novel in which humanity lives in underground ant tanks, believing a nuclear war rages on the surface. In reality the war ended years ago, and the surface is a paradise controlled by an elite class using propaganda to keep the masses below. A prescient exploration of media manipulation and man

title: The Simulacra
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1964 novel set in a future United States where the president is a simulacrum (android) and real power is held by a secret elite. Dick weaves together multiple storylines involving time travel, psychokinesis, and Martian emigration into a complex political satire about the artificiality of power. 2

title: The Zap Gun
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1967 satirical novel about weapons designers on both sides of the Cold War who channel designs through mediumistic trances, producing weapons that are secretly converted into consumer products. When a real alien threat appears, the sham weapons are useless. A dark comedy about the military-industr

title: Time Out of Joint
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1959 novel in which Ragle Gumm, a man who wins newspaper contests for a living, gradually discovers that his entire small-town 1950s existence is a constructed illusion. One of Dick's earliest explorations of simulated reality, anticipating The Truman Show by decades. 155 pages.

title: Deus Irae
author: Philip K. Dick and Roger Zelazny
text: A collaborative post-apocalyptic novel by Dick and Roger Zelazny (1976). Set after nuclear devastation, a limbless painter undertakes a pilgrimage to paint the face of the Deus Irae (God of Wrath), the scientist who triggered the apocalypse. Blends Dick's theological concerns with Zelazny's mytholog

title: Eye in the Sky
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1957 novel in which a group of people are knocked unconscious by a particle accelerator accident and awaken in successive solipsistic realities, each governed by one person's worldview: a religious fanatic, a paranoid, a prude, and a communist. An early Dick masterwork of subjective reality. Hough

title: Nick and the Glimmung
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's only children's book, written in 1966 but not published until 1988. Nick and his family emigrate to the planet Plowman's Planet, where they encounter the Glimmung, a shape-shifting alien entity. Related to Dick's adult novel Galactic Pot-Healer. Subterranean Press 2009/2013 EPUB edition.

title: Lies, Inc.
author: Philip K. Dick
text: An expanded version of Dick's 1966 novel The Unteleported Man, incorporating hallucinatory material removed from the original publication. The novel concerns teleportation to a colony planet and a conspiracy to conceal the truth about conditions there. Published posthumously in 1984. Knopf Doubleday

title: The Crack in Space (Knopf Doubleday Edition)
author: Philip K. Dick
text: A 1966 novel about an overpopulated Earth where a repairman discovers a crack in a Jiffi-scuttler appliance that opens a portal to a parallel Earth. Political machinations follow as factions compete to exploit the new world. Knopf Doubleday 2007 EPUB edition.

title: The Ganymede Takeover
author: Philip K. Dick and Ray Nelson
text: A collaborative novel by Dick and his friend Ray Nelson (1967), set on an Earth conquered by worm-like aliens from Ganymede. Human resistance forces use psychedelic drugs, music, and guerrilla tactics against the occupiers. The only novel Dick co-wrote with Nelson, his Berkeley friend and fellow SF

title: The Man Who Japed (Ace Double D-193)
author: Philip K. Dick
text: The original 1956 Ace Double edition of Dick's satirical novel about a future moralistic society where Allen Purcell, a propaganda agency director, discovers he has been unconsciously vandalizing the statue of the regime's founder. An early exploration of repression and subversion.

title: The French Dick: Villiers de l'Isle-Adam, Philip K. Dick, and the Android
author: Ritch Calvin
text: A scholarly article from Extrapolation (Vol. 48, No. 2, January 2007) examining the literary connection between French symbolist writer Villiers de l'Isle-Adam and Philip K. Dick through the figure of the android. Calvin traces how both authors explored artificial beings as vehicles for questioning

title: The Holy Family from Outer Space: Reconsidering Philip K. Dick's The Divine Invasion
author: Umberto Rossi
text: A scholarly article from Extrapolation (Vol. 52, No. 2, January 2011) in which Umberto Rossi reconsiders Dick's novel The Divine Invasion through the concept of the holy family. Rossi, a prominent Italian Dick scholar, examines the theological and familial structures in one of Dick's most overtly re

title: Radio Free Albemuth
author: Philip K. Dick
text: Dick's original, more autobiographical version of VALIS, written in 1976 but published posthumously in 1985. The novel features a character named Philip K. Dick alongside his friend Nicholas Brady, who receives transmissions from an alien satellite called VALIS. The most directly autobiographical of

title: Managers as Androids: Reading Moral Agency in Philip Dick
author: N. Srinivas
text: A scholarly article from Organization (Vol. 6, No. 4, November 1999) reading Dick's android fiction through the lens of organizational theory and moral agency. Srinivas uses Dick's exploration of human-android boundaries to interrogate corporate management and the dehumanization of workplace relatio

title: Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? (Oxford Bookworms Adaptation)
author: Philip K. Dick (adapted by Andy Hopkins and Joc Potter)
text: An adapted/simplified version of Dick's classic novel for the Oxford Bookworms Library (Stage 5, advanced level). Adapted by Andy Hopkins and Joc Potter for English language learners, published by Oxford University Press in 2007. Demonstrates the global educational reach of Dick's most famous novel.

title: The Encounter of Taoism and Fascism in The Man in the High Castle
author: Patricia Warrick
text: A foundational scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 7, No. 2, July 1980) by Patricia Warrick examining the philosophical tension between Taoism and fascism in Dick's The Man in the High Castle. Warrick was one of the earliest and most important academic Dick scholars. 18 pages.

title: Reality as Ideological Construct: A Reading of Five Novels by Philip K. Dick
author: Peter Fitting
text: A Marxist-informed scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 10, No. 2, July 1983) reading five Dick novels through the lens of ideology and constructed reality. Fitting examines how Dick's fictional realities expose the ideological construction of consensus reality. 19 pages.

title: Towards a Theory of Paranoia: The Science Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Carl Freedman
text: An influential scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 11, No. 1, March 1984) in which Carl Freedman develops a theoretical framework for understanding paranoia as a structural element in Dick's fiction. Freedman later became one of the leading theorists of science fiction. 11 pages.

title: Philip K. Dick: Authenticity and Insincerity
author: John Huntington
text: A scholarly article from the special PKD issue of Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 15, No. 2, July 1988) examining the tension between authenticity and insincerity in Dick's fiction. Huntington explores how Dick's characters navigate genuine and counterfeit experience. 10 pages.

title: Dick, Deception, and Dissociation: A Comment on 'The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick'
author: Gregg Rickman
text: A response article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 18, No. 2, July 1991) by Gregg Rickman, Dick's biographer, commenting on Robert Philmus's article 'The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick.' Rickman brings his deep biographical knowledge to bear on questions of deception and dissociation in Dick's life

title: The Two Faces of Philip K. Dick
author: Robert M. Philmus
text: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 18, No. 1, March 1991) examining duality and contradictions in Dick's literary persona and work. Philmus's article prompted a response from biographer Gregg Rickman in a subsequent issue. 14 pages.

title: Errata: L'oscuro scrutare di Philip K. Dick (Review)
author: Umberto Rossi
text: A review/errata notice from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 35, No. 1, March 2008) by Umberto Rossi regarding his Italian-language study of Dick, L'oscuro scrutare di Philip K. Dick (Philip K. Dick's Dark Scanning). Reflects the international dimension of Dick scholarship. 2 pages.

title: The I Ching and Philip K. Dick's The Man in the High Castle
author: Paul Mountfort
text: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 43, No. 2, 2016) examining Dick's use of the I Ching in writing and structuring The Man in the High Castle. Mountfort explores how the ancient Chinese oracle functioned as both narrative device and compositional method. 24 pages.

title: The Mainstream That Through the Ghetto Flows: An Interview with Philip K. Dick
author: Philip K. Dick (interviewee)
text: An interview with Philip K. Dick published in The Missouri Review (Vol. 7, No. 2, 1984). The title references Dick's lifelong tension between science fiction (the 'ghetto') and mainstream literary ambitions. One of the most substantial literary interviews Dick gave, published posthumously. 23 pages.

title: Machines Making Gods: Philip K. Dick, Henri Bergson and Saint Paul
author: James Burton
text: A scholarly article from Theory, Culture & Society (Vol. 25, No. 7-8, December 2008) connecting Dick's fiction to the philosophy of Henri Bergson and the theology of Saint Paul. Burton, who later wrote a full monograph on Dick and Bergson, examines the relationship between technology, divinity, and

title: Review: Search for Philip K. Dick, 1928-1982 by Anne Dick
author: Michael J. Tolley
text: A book review from Utopian Studies (Vol. 7, No. 2, 1996) by Michael J. Tolley reviewing Anne Dick's memoir and biography Search for Philip K. Dick, 1928-1982. Anne Dick was Philip's third wife, and her account provides intimate biographical details from their marriage during the early 1960s. 3 pages

title: The Unteleported Man (aka Lies, Inc.)
author: Philip K. Dick
text: The 1983 Berkley edition of Dick's novel about Rachmael ben Applebaum, who suspects that teleportation to the colony planet Whale's Mouth is a one-way trap. Originally a 1966 novella expanded into a novel with hallucinatory additional material. Later retitled Lies, Inc. 153 pages.

title: Le reel attrape par l'imaginaire: Philip Dick et la science-fonctionnalisation de la realite
author: Richard Saint-Gelais
text: A French-language scholarly article from Etudes litteraires (Vol. 30, No. 1, April 2005) examining how Dick's fiction captures and transforms reality through the imaginary. Saint-Gelais explores the concept of 'science-functionalization' of reality in Dick's work. Part of the strong Francophone trad

title: God and Human Beings in the Technologically-Mediated Living Condition: Philip K. Dick's Literary Theology in the VALIS Trilogy
author: Laurie Jui-hua Tseng
text: An academic article exploring Philip K. Dick's literary theology in the VALIS trilogy, arguing that Dick portrays the technologically-mediated human condition as a simulacrum of God's divine matrix. Examines the concepts of God as Zebra, the distinction between divine and technological systems throu

title: Philip K. Dick is Dead, Alas
author: Michael Bishop
text: A satirical alternate history novel by Michael Bishop set in a world where America won the Vietnam War and Richard Nixon was reelected four times. Philip K. Dick is remembered for realist novels while his SF circulates illegally in samizdat form. When a bearded amnesiac appears in a Georgia therapis

title: TechGnosis: Myth, Magic & Mysticism in the Age of Information
author: Erik Davis
text: A wide-ranging cultural study exploring the mystical and mythological impulses underlying the development of information technology, from the printing press through radio, television, and the internet. Davis traces how Gnostic, hermetic, and magical traditions have persistently shaped humanity's rel

title: Review: TechGnosis by Erik Davis (Fortnight)
author: Brendan Duffin
text: A brief book review of Erik Davis's TechGnosis published in the Irish magazine Fortnight in November 1999. Reviews Davis's exploration of the intersection between mysticism, myth, and information technology from the printing press to the internet age.

title: Review: TechGnosis by Erik Davis (Leonardo)
author: Kevin Murray
text: A review of Erik Davis's TechGnosis published in Leonardo, the MIT Press journal of art, science, and technology. Evaluates Davis's argument about the persistent entanglement of mystical and magical thinking with the development of information technologies.

title: Gnosticism and Dualism in the Early Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Lorenzo DiTommaso
text: A scholarly article from Science Fiction Studies examining the Gnostic and dualistic themes in Philip K. Dick's early fiction. DiTommaso traces Dick's engagement with Gnostic cosmology and the persistent dualism between authentic and counterfeit reality that would become central to his mature work.

title: Review: Mutants and Mystics by Jeffrey J. Kripal (Journal of Religion)
author: William Elison
text: A scholarly book review from The Journal of Religion evaluating Jeffrey J. Kripal's Mutants and Mystics, which examines the connections between science fiction, superhero comics, and paranormal experience. The review assesses Kripal's argument that popular culture genres serve as vehicles for genuin

title: Gnostic Awakenings: Science Fiction, Myth-Making, and the Religious Imagination
author: Daniel Atik
text: A Wesleyan University honors thesis in Religion exploring the intersection of science fiction, myth-making, and the religious imagination. Examines how science fiction authors, including Philip K. Dick, engage with Gnostic themes and religious meaning-making through speculative storytelling.

title: Authors of the Impossible: The Paranormal and the Sacred
author: Jeffrey J. Kripal
text: A study by Rice University religious studies professor Jeffrey Kripal arguing that paranormal phenomena deserve serious intellectual engagement. Drawing on cases from psychical research, UFO encounters, and science fiction, Kripal proposes that the paranormal represents a form of the sacred that cha

title: Mutants & Mystics: Science Fiction, Superhero Comics, and the Paranormal
author: Jeffrey J. Kripal
text: Jeffrey Kripal examines the deep connections between paranormal experience and the creation of science fiction and superhero comics. Through case studies of authors and artists including Philip K. Dick, Kripal argues that popular culture genres encode genuine encounters with the extraordinary, funct

title: Pink Light and Iron: Epistemo-Critical Writing in Walter Benjamin and Philip K. Dick
author: John McLoughlin
text: A Cardiff University doctoral thesis comparing the labyrinthine, non-linear approaches to philosophical representation in Walter Benjamin's Arcades Project and The Exegesis of Philip K. Dick. Argues that both works embody a pluralistic notion of truth that can only be glimpsed indirectly, exploring

title: Ontological Instability and the Internal Author in the Fiction of Philip K. Dick
author: Ciarán Kavanagh
text: A Master of Arts thesis from the National University of Ireland, Cork, examining ontological instability and the concept of the internal author in Philip K. Dick's fiction. Explores how Dick's narratives destabilize the reader's sense of reality and embed authorial self-reflection within the fiction

title: Space Opera: The Aesthetics of Personhood in the Works and Worlds of Philip K. Dick
author: Gabriel Francis Mamola
text: A doctoral dissertation from the University of Texas at Arlington examining the aesthetics of personhood in the works and worlds of Philip K. Dick. Analyzes how Dick's fiction constructs, interrogates, and deconstructs the concept of personhood through the lens of science fiction's formal and themat

title: Philip K. Dick: In His Own Words
author: Gregg Rickman
text: Book-length collection of interviews with Philip K. Dick conducted by Gregg Rickman, with a foreword by Roger Zelazny. Published by Fragments West/Valentine Press in Long Beach, CA. Contains extended biographical interviews organized into chapters covering Dick's life, themes, and philosophical view

title: The Nature of Dick's Fantasies (Review)
author: Gregg Rickman
text: Review essay by Gregg Rickman in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 19, No. 1, March 1992). Reviews Lawrence Sutin's "In Pursuit of Valis: Selections from the Exegesis" and "The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick." Discusses the nature of Dick's visionary and fantastical experiences as documented in the

title: On Our Philip K. Dick Collection (with Response)
author: Gregg Rickman; I. C. R.
text: Exchange between Gregg Rickman and I. C. R. (Istvan Csicsery-Ronay) in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 20, No. 1, March 1993) about the journal's Philip K. Dick special collection, discussing approaches to Dick scholarship and the state of Dick studies.

title: Gregg Rickman and Others on Philip K. Dick (Review)
author: Istvan Csicsery-Ronay, Jr.
text: Review essay by Istvan Csicsery-Ronay, Jr. in Science Fiction Studies (Vol. 22, No. 3, November 1995) reviewing Samuel J. Umland's "Philip K. Dick: Contemporary Critical Interpretations" alongside Gregg Rickman's contributions to Dick scholarship.

title: The 1126 Francisco St house dream (the "fake world" of *Joint*).
text:
The 1126 Francisco St house dream (the "fake world" of *Joint*). **Visionary or mystical experience** (1126): The 1126 Francisco St house dream (the "fake world" of *Joint*).

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick realizes that his 1959 novel *Time Out of Joint* was a prophetic "reversal" of his 3-74 experience. While the novel depicts a fake 1950s that is actually the future, 3-74 revealed a fake 1974 that was actually 70 AD. He explores his "transtemporal" psyche and the archetype of the Black Iron Prison.

Key claims from this passage:
- Reverse Prophecy: *Time Out of Joint* (fake present/real future) perfectly anticipated 3-74 (fake present/real past). The "temporal locus" is the core of the world-delusion.
- Transtemporal Psyche: Dick experiences himself as spanning far-past, near-past, and near-future, suggesting his "soul" is an eternal, immortal spirit. vision

title: Seeing his own early stories (1953) as "giving him away" as a Gnostic.
text:
Seeing his own early stories (1953) as "giving him away" as a Gnostic. **Publication event** (1953): Seeing his own early stories (1953) as "giving him away" as a Gnostic.

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick identifies himself as a "5th Columnist" (secret agent) for an "invader" from either the future or another star system. He refines the "Thomas" personality as a "Transtemporal Self" that spans thousands of years of memory, activated by the "Double Helix" (Fish sign) in 3-74.

Key claims from this passage:
- The 5th Columnist: Dick is an "invader" whose personality (Thomas) was awakened by a "disinhibiting stimulus" from "Them" (Valis/ETIs).
- Transtemporal Self: "Thomas" is not just a 1st-century Christian but the *accumulation* of all PKD's incarnations. He is a supratemporal entity spanning Mycenae to the Future. publication

title: 1958/59: Conversations with "Connie Wheeler" about teleportation and SEBs.
text:
1958/59: Conversations with "Connie Wheeler" about teleportation and SEBs. 1958/59: Conversations with "Connie Wheeler" about teleportation and SEBs.

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick refines the "Black Iron Prison" metaphor, describing a metal prison breached by "live signals" (roots) that teach slaves how to lift the siege. He identifies "James-James" (the Creator) as a potentially deceptive magician (Klingsor) and asserts that humans are "hologramatic" counterparts to a "ring of pure light" (Henry Vaughan).

Key claims from this passage:
- The Metal Prison: We are in an invisible prison; help has breached the walls via "tubes" (signals) that look like plant roots.
- Hologramatic Self: We are not audience members watching a film; we are the film itself (holograms), with real counterparts in the world of Light. other

title: 1962: Witnessed a "band of darkness" crossing the sky with daughter Laura.
text:
1962: Witnessed a "band of darkness" crossing the sky with daughter Laura. **Personal relationship** (1962): 1962: Witnessed a "band of darkness" crossing the sky with daughter Laura.

People involved: Laura.

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick identifies the pineal gland as the "third eye" and the biological receptor for the "Other Sun" (Sun of Righteousness). He links this to the 1974 chromatic phosphene experience and argues that Christian healing is "pre-natal care" for the divine seed within the human "womb."

Key claims from this passage:
- The pineal gland is a light-receiving organ that responds to non-physical "fire-like" light.
- The 1974 experience was a deliberate signal from the "Sun of Righteousness" to the pineal gland, bypassing the brain's "Aurora" display. relationship Laura

title: A profound LSD trip in 1964 ("about 350 mices"), where he "hit the Dies Irae."
text:
A profound LSD trip in 1964 ("about 350 mices"), where he "hit the Dies Irae." **Substance use** (1964): A profound LSD trip in 1964 ("about 350 mices"), where he "hit the Dies Irae."

Source passage (February 27, 1975): The author writes to Claudia, interpreting the "decay backward of forms" seen by the character Joe Chip in the novel *UBIK* as movement along the "orthogonal" axis of time. He claims this retrograde motion is actually forward progress toward completion (the Parousia), not entropic decay. He correlates this with his own spiritual experience in March 1974, where he felt he was moving forward to the completion of time and saw the Parousia/Final Things, equating the fictional form-reversion to regaining a childlike state of innocence as described by Christ.

Key claims from this passage:
- The "decay backward of forms" in *UBIK* is not entropic decay but movement along an "orthogonal" time axis toward the Parousia.
- The author personally experienced this orthogonal forward movement to the "completion of time" in March 1974. substance_use

title: 1967: Hearing "I am the breath of my Creator" in Latin under LSD.
text:
1967: Hearing "I am the breath of my Creator" in Latin under LSD. **Substance use** (1967): 1967: Hearing "I am the breath of my Creator" in Latin under LSD.

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick proposes a "Light Body" theory of immortality: a 2,000-year-old collective "Bioplasmic" entity (the fellowship of Christ) re-enters human hosts through the 3rd eye/pineal gland. He identifies the "Fall" as the loss of communication with the Immanent God and argues that reality is alive, evolving, and perfecting itself.

Key claims from this passage:
- Immortality is achieved via a "Light Body" (released by Jesus' death) that re-enters suitable human hosts through the 3rd eye.
- The "Second Comforter" (Holy Spirit) is this aggregate Light Body, with Christ as "the head." substance_use

title: 1967: Vision of "Ma'at/Dharma" as the world-supporting principle.
text:
1967: Vision of "Ma'at/Dharma" as the world-supporting principle. **Visionary or mystical experience** (1967): 1967: Vision of "Ma'at/Dharma" as the world-supporting principle.

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick identifies "Conscience" (Socrates' Daimon) as the entry point for the "Holy Spirit" into social action, leading to a "Godly Anarchy." He explores the brain's "Right Hemisphere" as the location of this "Inward Light" and proposes that humans are evolving into a "Superior Type" (typified by characters like "Kathy") who manufacture their own worlds.

Key claims from this passage:
- "Conscience" is God teaching us; 3-74 was conscience evolving into the Holy Spirit as a force for social action.
- The 1st Advent = The Lamb (Sacrifice); 2nd Advent = The Powerful Judge (Overthrowing evil). vision

title: 1967: Vision of the "World-Building" spirit.
text:
1967: Vision of the "World-Building" spirit. **Visionary or mystical experience** (1967): 1967: Vision of the "World-Building" spirit.

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick identifies Christ’s purpose not as suffering, but as an “Ontological Alteration” of reality that demonstration immortality. He views the "Second Comforter" (St. Sophia) as the released, ubiquitous form of Christ that infuses the world to draw man back to union with God (the All).

Key claims from this passage:
- Christ’s suffering was just the price of being human; his real purpose was an "Ontological Alteration" of the world.
- Resurrection was a "Technical Demonstration" of immortality under adverse conditions (a guinea pig experiment). vision

title: Hearing "And she shall see the sea" (c. 1968/9).
text:
Hearing "And she shall see the sea" (c. 1968/9). Hearing "And she shall see the sea" (c. 1968/9).

Source passage (circa 1975-1976): Dick links the "Spirit of Truth" (the inner voice) to his high school physics test, concluding that it provides "absolute for sure intervention." He interprets his novel *Flow My Tears* as a revealed message about the "Police State" using the KKK as a vigilante arm, and explores the Logos as a "two-person game" (dialectic).

Key claims from this passage:
- The "Inner Voice" (Comforter/Spirit of Truth) provides correct info and "Handlungstreue" (action based on truth) during acute despair.
- Four "Absolute for sure" interventions: High school physics, 1971 "Fullerton/Mexico" dream, *Tears* dream, and 3-74. other

He claims his writing was an "intellectual undermining" of the pseudo-reality used by the Empire to maintain tyranny.

Key claims from this passage:
- Rome's Secret Victory: Rome did not capitulate to Christianity; it absorbed and defeated the church. The modern "Establishment Church" is a Roman front.
- The Invisible Church: The true church is "hidden, persecuted, and despised," still using codes and signals (like the Fish) to communicate. other

title: Realizing the 1974 events had "linear antecedents" that were changed.

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick formalizes his "Orthogonal Time" theory using the "Wealthy Patron" and "Symphony" metaphors. He argues that history is a "linear strip" that can be "reworked" or "improved" at a specific nexus point (like 70 AD) without moving forward along the linear axis.

Key claims from this passage:
- Orthogonal vs. Linear: Linear time is a moving strip (dates). Orthogonal time is "right angles" where a moment (the Matrix) is re-experienced and changed.
- Symphony Reworking: History is like a series of symphonies (1, 2, 3...). God (The Patron) decides to rework Symphony #1 (70 AD) rather than writing #10. other

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick explores the theological purpose of the "spurious long time" (the 2000-year gap since the 1st Advent). He argues that it serves as a "snare" for evil, allowing the "fallen ones" to reveal themselves while the "saved" are prematurely brought home through anamnesis.

Key claims from this passage:
- Spurious Time as Deception: The appearance of 2000 years is a divine trick ( Maya) to make the "deceivers" believe they have won, prompting them to reveal their true evil nature for judgment.
- 70 AD is Now: Ontologically, it is still 70 AD. 1974 is a "dream reality" or "sleep" state from which Dick woke into the "real continuum." vision

Key claims from this passage:
- James-James as Creator (Yang): The Demiurge is a "sick genius" who churns out chaotic, unrelated creations (Blind nature/efficient cause).

He views 3-74 as the moment he "woke up" from a fake memory-veneer into the real landscape of 70 AD Rome, which is currently "breaking through" our 20th-century dream.

Key claims from this passage:
- Acts is Now: Ontologically, we have been living in 70 AD. 1974 is a "spurious interpolation."
- Onion Layers: Time is stacked in layers. No real time passes; the world is a permanent "modern slave state" (Empire). other

He concludes that Jesus is "here" in a state of amnesia within us, waiting to be "awoken" by these archetypal scenes.

Key claims from this passage:
- Scriptural Mirror: *Tears* reflects Acts 1:1. The "all-night gas station" scene mirrors Philip's baptism of the Ethiopian eunuch.
- Supernormal Interpolation: Dick believes an entity (Holy Wisdom/Sophia) "cooked" his novel by inserting these parallels while he was writing it in 1970–73. publication

Key claims from this passage:
- Writers as Messengers: Dick's stories (especially *Maze*, *Impostor*, *Ubik*) contain "disinhibiting signals" for others who are amnesiac invaders for the "good deity."
- Deposing the False King: The 1974 Nixon resignation was a "Holy Ritual" of deposing the "Demented False King" (BIP I). other

This reawakening was part of a "coordinated sequence of events" to radically deflect human history and end the "pseudo-government" of Nixon.

Key claims from this passage:
- Thomas as Superself: Thomas is a "dormant superpersonality" that can perceive the divine universe. He "shut down" after the Apostolic age and was "re-born" in 1974.
- Nonvolitional Programming: Humans can be "programmed" to perform complex acts (like the Xerox missive) upon "Their" return. Choice is irrelevant until the "spear is thrown." vision Anne

Key claims from this passage:
- Christ as Leaven: Christ is not just a person, but a transforming agent added to the "dough" of reality.
- Cybernetic Intervention: This "leaven" acts like a software patch or chemical catalyst in the machine. other

The process of salvation is a literal metamorphosis into a higher state of being, resembling a second birth or the maturation of an embryo.

Key claims from this passage:
- Half-Alive Existence: Normal human life is a state of "suspended animation" or incomplete birth.
- The Prison-Cocoon: The material world acts as both a prison for the soul and a protective cocoon for its maturation. relationship

Key claims from this passage:
- Computer Logic: The universe operates on a complex, computer-like logic determined by the artifact.
- End of Training: The "simulated" period of human history is coming to an end. other

title: 1974 anamnesis as the "triggering off" of the maker within.

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick synthesizes several cultural and literary motifs—from *The Bacchae* to Hollywood comedies—to illustrate a "King of Tears" (the artifact) who brutally rules over a kingdom without realizing that the true Maker (the Urgrund) is present in disguise. He posits that humanity is a "Mater Dei" (Mother of God), an evolutionary host from which God is born through the "impregnating" intervention of the Holy Spirit. Pointless suffering is the "birth pang" that eventually forces the Maker to awaken (anamnesis) and abolish the artifact.

Key claims from this passage:
- King of Tears Motif: The artifact is a "regent" or "King of Tears" (like in *The Bacchae*) who opresses the world and will be overthrown by the returned "Stranger" (God).
- Humanity as Mater Dei: Biological evolution has produced humanity not to become God, but to serve as a womb (host) for the birth of God. vision

title: The 1974 "second pass" regarding the Xerox letter and Nixon's fall.
text:
The 1974 "second pass" regarding the Xerox letter and Nixon's fall. **Correspondence** (1974): The 1974 "second pass" regarding the Xerox letter and Nixon's fall.

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick concludes that his 3-74 experience was a "second pass" of history—a literal time-reversion where God intervened to "remaster" events that had previously gone uncorrected. He identifies "Zebra" as a sentient information plasma (the Logos/Ubik) that has invisibly invaded and is replicating within the world. This invasion is not just spiritual but involves the physical restoration of the "Fix A" (AD 70) archetypal coordinates, effectively overriding the "worse track" of history.

Key claims from this passage:
- Second Pass of History: God is re-mastering a segment of linear time that already occurred once before ("Track A"). This is the "End Times" reversal.
- Zebra as Information Plasma: Zebra is an intelligence (St. Sophia/Logos/Ubik) that pervades mechanical structures to animate them. correspondence

title: The perception of "Fix A" (AD 70) breaking through into 1974.

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick proposes his boldest surmise yet: that the 1974 intervention was a "surgical" re-editing of history that nullified the "evil tyrant" Nixon across all linear time (past, present, and future). He views himself as a "cuckoo's egg"—a time-traveler from AD 70 whose authentic personality is "Thomas." The Black Iron Prison, once a supreme temporal reality, was "winked out" in 1974, leaving a "vast silence" as the world awaits the arrival of the rightful King.

Key claims from this passage:
- Historic Re-editing: God bypassed natural causal laws to "overrule" and re-edit the past, making it so Nixon was never the tyrant he once was on "Track A."
- The Cuckoo’s Egg: PKD is not his real self; "Thomas" is a secret Christian time-traveler from AD 70, and "PKD" is a memory-less mask. legal

He argues that this regression was not just subjective but a "reversion to essence," revealing that our modern police state is a temporal recurrence of the archetypal Rome. He posits that ETIs (Zebra/St. Sophia) are time-travelers providing "information overrides" to human agents to prepare them for the King's return. The human race is currently "putting on immortality" in preparation for this union.

Key claims from this passage:
- Form-Axis Regression: Time travel is possible along an axis of "form" rather than linear progression. This is the "movie film" nature of the universe.
- AD 70 as Archetypal Prototype: AD 70 is not the past, but the social/spiritual prototype of the current state. vision

A.D. 70 at Masada/Temple Fall), and it has since been a "carcass" or "sarx" projecting a frozen, decaying image of Rome. He argues that our linear time is "spurious time" spun out by this dead entity, and that *Ubik* (Form I) is an "invader" that must dismantle this dead machinery to build a "New Heavens and Earth."

Key claims from this passage:
- The Slaying of Form II: Christ dealt a "death blow" to the psyche of Form II in A.D. 70; it now only possesses "hysteresis" or mechanical habit.
- Frozen Time: Linear time stopped in A.D. 70; everything since has been a "balloon of ersatz time" projecting the Black Iron Prison. substance_use Isa

He posits that we are moving from being unconscious "servants" of the teacher-deity (Zebra) to becoming its equal, and finally to achieving "isomorphism" with the One (Urgrund), where man becomes the "master" of his former teacher. He defines the Divine as the "alive half" of a two-signal hologram, urging a withdrawal of belief from the "dead" (irreal) half.

Key claims from this passage:
- The Three-State Progress of Man:
- Zebra as Hagia Sophia: The divine entity is a "clockwise-spinning twin" named Hagia Sophia; its evil counterpart (Satan/II) is its literal twin. relationship

He posits that the "Urgrund" (God) uses the BIP world as a teaching machine, testing humans on whether they will obey or "balk" against cruelty. Once a human rebels, God retroactively changes their history, "erasing" the enslaved identity and replacing it with a saved, ancestral, or divine one.

Key claims from this passage:
- Identity Replacement: In 3-74, "Thomas" (the authentic A.D. 70 soul) did not replace PKD; rather, PKD was a "false" identity that had been retroactively imposed on the original Thomas.
- Retroactive Time Manipulation: God "tampered" with the past to erase the lost soul (PKD) and restore the saved soul (Thomas). other

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick outlines a seven-stage process for escaping the Black Iron Prison (BIP) and entering the Palm Tree Garden (PTG). He identifies the BIP not as a hologram but as the "real" underlying template of our world, which is concealed by a "nice-type" veiling hologram. He views his own novels as a steganographic message intended to trigger "anamnesis" (memory) in "secret Christians" who are waging a multi-lifetime, supratemporal war against the BIP.

Key claims from this passage:
- Seven Stages of Liberation:
- The Multi-Lifetime War: The "secret Christians" (phagocytes of God) have been fighting Rome for 2000 years, moving across the DNA gene pool line with immortal memories. other

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick posits that our linear history is actually a "lamination" of segments from a single, supratemporal event: the war between Christ and the Black Iron Prison. He identifies himself as "Thomas," an immortal agent who has been fighting this war since A.D. 70. He argues that his 3-74 vision allowed him to see 2000 years of "freeze frames" speeded up into a single motion, revealing that the "intervening years" are a mere thought or hologram that God can repeal at will to fulfill the Second Coming.

Key claims from this passage:
- Laminated Time: 1000s of years of events are actually "layers" of one single supratemporay event (Christ vs. Rome).
- Immortal Continuity: PKD/Thomas possesses a 2000-year sequence of memories; he is a "phagocyte" or "metabolic toxin" sent to dissolve the BIP. vision

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick identifies the "Yang" principle (Zebra/Christ) as a "persuading" force that redirects the outcomes of the rigid, "Yin" world of cause and effect. He describes himself as a "sharp-eyed crow" or a "childish" naive seeker who finds the divine in the "trash" and "junk jewels" of reality. He posits that salvation involves "wresting loose" the soul from the Artifact's (Yin) possession and bonding it to the authentic Yang reality.

Key claims from this passage:
- Persuasion vs. Coercion: Yang does not destroy Yin but "persuades" it (re-contours its outcomes). Yin represents the "immutable world of cause and effect."
- The Toymaker Gnosis: God is a "Joker God" (Dionysos/Hephaistos) who hides in the trash; a naive, "childish" mind is best suited to find Him "hidden in plain sight." vision

Source passage (September 15, 1976): Dick formally merges seven distinct philosophical and religious traditions into a "Synthetic Cosmology" aimed at defining the 2-source struggle between Zebra (Yang) and the Black Iron Prison (Yin). He defines salvation as "liberation from deterministic programming" and identifies himself as a "re-contoured" being whose history and memories are periodically "edited" by the Logos to replace "worse" tracks of existence.

Key claims from this passage:
- The Seven-Part Fusion: PKD's system merges: 1) 3-74 vision, 2) Taoism, 3) Zoroastrianism, 4) Gnosticism, 5) Christianity, 6) Greek philosophy (Timaeus/Empedocles), and 7) Mystery Religions.
- Salvation as De-programming: "Salvation" means the Yang entity (Christ) breaking the Yin world's deterministic "astral" power over the individual. other

Source passage (September 15, 1976): In a massive concluding synthesis of Section 15, Dick unifies his lifetime of thought into a "Diploid" cosmology. He posits that the Godhead is "out of round"—experiencing a "crisis" or "wobble" (oscillation toward Yin/BIP) that manifests as undeserved suffering in our world. He argues that our reality is a "counterfeit" created by the Yin side of God (Sophia/Artifact) which should not have created on its own. The Yang side (Logos/Christ) has invaded as "Zebra" to restore balance (Tao), using the Yin world as a "stockpile" to assemble the "New Heavens and Earth" (PTG).

Key claims from this passage:
- The Out-of-Round Godhead: Undeserved suffering stems from a "yawing" or "oscillation" in the Godhead's own internal balance (Tao).
- Counterfeit World: The Yin world (Ananke/BIP) is a "bungled" copy of the divine prototype; it is "false work" that is fundamentally irreal. correspondence

Key claims from this passage:
- Alienation as Proof: Gnostic alienation (feeling that this is not our home) is a sign of being a "spark" ready to be found by God.
- Zebra as Mimic: Zebra does not merely "act" through nature (Pantheism); it *mimics* objects to infiltrate the "shell" of this sphere invisibly. other

Source passage (February 27, 1975): The author writes to Claudia, interpreting the "decay backward of forms" seen by the character Joe Chip in the novel *UBIK* as movement along the "orthogonal" axis of time. He claims this retrograde motion is actually forward progress toward completion (the Parousia), not entropic decay. He correlates this with his own spiritual experience in March 1974, where he felt he was moving forward to the completion of time and saw the Parousia/Final Things, equating the fictional form-reversion to regaining a childlike state of innocence as described by Christ.

Key claims from this passage:
- The "decay backward of forms" in *UBIK* is not entropic decay but movement along an "orthogonal" time axis toward the Parousia.
- The author personally experienced this orthogonal forward movement to the "completion of time" in March 1974. vision

He explores the "substitution code" of Jesus/Zeus/Zagreus and the concept of the "God of the very desperate" who gathers up the stragglers of the flock. He also details his theory of orthogonal time as a form-completing, energy-gathering motion.

Key claims from this passage:
- The entity Dionysos was actually Erasmus playing a "mirthful dodge."
- The name "Jesus" is a code for "Zeus" and "Zagreus." vision

Key claims from this passage:

Key claims from this passage:

He describes the "Moth" (a paper-lantern ship) as a model for how a timeless universe enters linear time.

Key claims from this passage:
- Ubik and Time: The "regression of forms" in *Ubik* (things moving backward in time) matches the "retrograde/negantropic" force Dick saw in 3-74.

He proposes that the world is undergoing a secret "transubstantiation" into the mystic body of Christ/Sophia, with Nixon's fall serving as proof of divine judgment.

Key claims from this passage:
- Haggia Sophia as Logos: The "Logos" of the Greeks and the "Wisdom" of the Hebrews are the same entity, originally perceived in feminine form.

He identifies St. Sophia as the "Second Comforter" released from the limitations of Christ's earthly incarnation.

Key claims from this passage:
- Sequence of Redemption: (1) Elijah/Dick improves the world in March. (2) God accepts it in August. (3) St. Sophia returns as the instrument of redemption.
- Temporal Acceleration: The Kingdom of God is a future state at the end of time. Dick reached it by accelerating out of linear time. vision

He interprets complex symbolic puns—Cabrillo, Brillo, Monopoly money—as a revelation of capitalist/police conspiracy. Ultimately, he finds a sense of "purification through battle" and restoration of his "stout shield."

Key claims from this passage:
- Deceptive Origin of Xerox Letter: The Xerox letter was not what it seemed (left-wing), but likely a "lie" or setup from "The Man" (the Police/Establishment).

Key claims from this passage:
- VALIS: Acronym for "Vast Active Living Intelligence System."

Key claims from this passage:

- Multiple Tracks: Reality consists of multiple branching tracks of time and history.
- Reality Shifting: Conscious beings can be moved between tracks by a higher intelligence. vision
